title: Primary Keys & Unique Identifiers
subtitle: Every Record Needs Its Own ID Card!
overview: Students dive deep into the concept of primary keys — what they are, why every database table needs one, and how they keep data organised and conflict-free. Through relatable real-world examples like Aadhaar numbers, roll numbers and UPI IDs, students understand why uniqueness matters in databases.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — whiteboard table drawings, primary key diagrams, student activity sheets, SQL demos.

keyTopics: What is a Primary Key? — A column that uniquely identifies every row in a table
Rules of a Primary Key — Must be UNIQUE, must NOT be NULL, only ONE per table
Real-World Examples — Aadhaar number, Roll number, UPI ID, Employee ID, ISBN
Why Primary Keys Matter — Prevent duplicate records, enable fast lookups
AUTO_INCREMENT — Let SQL automatically assign unique ID numbers
NULL — What it means and why primary keys cannot be NULL
Composite Key — Using TWO columns together as a primary key
Foreign Key (Introduction) — How primary keys link two tables together
SQL Syntax — CREATE TABLE with PRIMARY KEY constraint
Common Mistakes — Using names or emails as primary keys (bad idea!)

activity.title: Design a Student Database with Primary Keys
steps: On paper, draw a Students table — columns: StudentID, Name, Class, Age, City
Fill in 5 rows with student data — two students can have the same name
Circle the PRIMARY KEY column — which one is always unique?
Try writing two rows with the same StudentID — what problem does this cause?
Open SQLiteOnline.com and create the table with PRIMARY KEY constraint
Use AUTO_INCREMENT so SQL assigns StudentID automatically
INSERT 5 student records — notice IDs are assigned automatically
Try inserting a duplicate StudentID manually — observe the error!
Challenge: Add a second table 'Classes' and link it using StudentID as a foreign key

quiz.q: What is a PRIMARY KEY in a database table?
options: The first column in any table
A column that uniquely identifies every row in the table
The most important piece of data in a row
A password to access the database
quiz.explanation: A Primary Key uniquely identifies every row! No two rows can share the same primary key value — it's like a unique ID card for every record in the table.

quiz.q: Which of these is the BEST real-world example of a primary key?
options: A student's Aadhaar number or Roll Number
quiz.explanation: Aadhaar number and Roll Number are perfect primary key examples — they are unique for every person and never repeated. Names and cities can be shared by many people!

quiz.q: What are the TWO most important rules of a primary key?
options: Must be a number and must be long
Must be UNIQUE and must NOT be NULL
Must be the first column and must be text
Must be entered by the user and must be short
quiz.explanation: Primary keys must be UNIQUE (no two rows share the same value) and NOT NULL (every row must have a value). These two rules guarantee every record can be found!

quiz.q: What does NULL mean in a database?
options: The value is empty or unknown — no value at all
quiz.explanation: NULL means no value — not zero, not empty string, but completely absent. Primary keys cannot be NULL because every row must be identifiable.

quiz.q: Why can't you use a student's NAME as a primary key?
options: Names are too long for databases
Two students can have the same name — names are not always unique
quiz.explanation: Names are not unique! A class could have two students both named 'Riya' or 'Arjun'. A primary key must be guaranteed unique — so always use an ID number instead.

quiz.q: What does AUTO_INCREMENT do in SQL?
options: Automatically assigns the next unique number as the primary key for each new row
quiz.explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT means SQL handles ID assignment for you! First record gets ID 1, second gets ID 2, and so on — you never have to manually enter or track IDs.

quiz.q: What happens if you try to INSERT a row with a PRIMARY KEY value that already exists?
options: SQL replaces the old row silently
SQL adds both rows and assigns new IDs
SQL throws a Duplicate Key error and rejects the insert
SQL adds the row but marks it as a copy
quiz.explanation: SQL strictly enforces primary key uniqueness! If you try to insert a duplicate key value, you get a 'Duplicate entry' error and the row is rejected. This protects your data integrity.

quiz.q: How many PRIMARY KEYS can a single table have?
options: Only one primary key (but it can span multiple columns)
quiz.explanation: Each table can only have ONE primary key. However, that key can be made up of multiple columns — called a Composite Key — when a single column isn't enough to guarantee uniqueness.

options: A key made of numbers and letters mixed together
A primary key that uses TWO or more columns together to uniquely identify a row
A key that is shared between two tables
quiz.explanation: A Composite Key combines two or more columns to form a unique identifier. Example: In an exam results table, StudentID + SubjectID together uniquely identify each result row.

quiz.q: Which SQL keyword is used to define a primary key when creating a table?
quiz.explanation: PRIMARY KEY is the correct SQL keyword! Example: StudentID INT PRIMARY KEY — this tells SQL that StudentID is the unique identifier for every row in this table.

quiz.q: A FOREIGN KEY in one table refers to the _______ in another table.
quiz.explanation: A Foreign Key is a column in one table that points to the Primary Key of another table — creating a link between the two tables. This is how relational databases connect related data!

quiz.q: Why is using an email address as a primary key a bad idea?
options: Emails contain the @ symbol which SQL cannot store
People can change their email address, breaking the primary key
SQL does not support text primary keys
quiz.explanation: Primary keys should never change! Emails can be updated or transferred to someone else. If you use email as a primary key and it changes, all linked data breaks. Always prefer stable numeric IDs.

quiz.q: In a school database, which column would make the BEST primary key for a Students table?
quiz.explanation: StudentID is the best choice — it's a unique number assigned to each student that never changes or repeats. Name, Class and City can all be shared by multiple students.

quiz.q: What is the main benefit of having a primary key in every table?
options: It makes the table look more professional
It guarantees every record can be uniquely found, updated or deleted without confusion
It speeds up the computer's processor
It allows the table to store more data
quiz.explanation: Primary keys ensure every record is uniquely identifiable! Without them, you might accidentally update or delete the wrong record when two rows look similar.

quiz.q: Which real-world system uses a unique ID similar to a database primary key for every transaction?
options: UPI payment — every transaction gets a unique Transaction ID
quiz.explanation: Every UPI transaction gets a unique Transaction ID — just like a primary key! This ID lets banks track, verify and reference any specific payment without confusion.
